output:
# Resolving the HAZE Gap in Federated Newton Sketch (FedNS/FedNDES) with FedSANS: Federated Spectrum-Adaptive Newton Sketch

## 1. Background and Current State of the Art

The defining tension in federated learning (FL) is a tradeoff between the convergence rate and the communication burden: every improvement in how fast the global model reaches a target accuracy must be paid for in bytes moved across a bandwidth-constrained, high-latency network of clients. First-order methods set the baseline on the cheap-per-round side of this tradeoff. FedAvg / LocalSGD [1] communicate only a model-sized vector (O(M) per client per round) and amortize communication by running several local SGD steps before each synchronization; FedProx [2] adds a proximal term to stabilize those local steps under system and statistical heterogeneity. The cost of this per-round cheapness is convergence speed: under non-IID client data, local updates drift toward divergent local optima (client drift), and the number of communication rounds to a fixed accuracy inflates. Because the relevant resource is total bytes = (rounds) x (payload per round), a method that is cheap per round but needs many more rounds can be strictly worse end-to-end than one that pays more per round and finishes in far fewer.

This is precisely the opening that second-order methods exploit. In the distributed setting, DANE [18], GIANT, and related distributed-Newton schemes use curvature to take steps that are far less sensitive to heterogeneity and ill-conditioning, converging in dramatically fewer rounds than first-order baselines. Their obstacle is the cost of curvature: forming or inverting a full M x M Hessian is O(M^3) compute and O(M^2) communication, which is prohibitive. The Newton Sketch line [10] removes this obstacle by replacing the exact Hessian with a randomized low-dimensional sketch S H^{1/2} of its square root, where S is a k x N sketching matrix; the Newton step is then computed from a k x M sketched factor rather than the full Hessian, and approximation quality is governed by the sketch dimension k. The sharp characterization of how large k must be is the effective dimension d~_lambda = tr(H(H+lambda I)^{-1}), which counts the directions of curvature that actually matter at regularization level lambda; effective-dimension-adaptive sketching [11] sizes the sketch to d~_lambda rather than to the ambient M, so that a spectrally-simple problem is sketched cheaply and a spectrally-rich one is sketched accurately.

FedNS and FedNDES [9] are the federated instantiation of this idea, and they are the immediate state of the art for second-order FL. FedNS has each client sketch its LOCAL square-root Hessian, S^t_j H^{1/2}_{Dj,t} of size k x n_j, in an independent serialization; because the global square-root Hessian factor concatenates the local ones (S^t H^{1/2}_{D,t} = sum_j S^t_j H^{1/2}_{Dj,t}), summing the locally sketched blocks at the server RECOVERS the centralized Newton update exactly, with no client needing to expose raw Hessians. FedNS drives its sketch with an iteration-dependent accuracy eps_t = 1/log(1+t) and a sketch size lower-bounded by a squared Gaussian width <= min{N,M}; since N > M in FL, this forces k >~ M. FedNDES instead sketches the GLOBAL Hessian directly, uses backtracking line-search parameters (a,b), and employs TWO-STAGE sketch sizes m1, m2 tied to the effective dimension d~_lambda to achieve SUPERLINEAR (delta-accurate) convergence with a bounded total iteration count T. In both methods the per-round uplink payload is dominated not by the O(M) model vector but by the sketched-Hessian block, roughly k x M per client; the sketch IS the communication cost.

Two surrounding bodies of work bracket where these methods operate. On one side, the non-IID theory of FL [6][7][5][8] establishes that client heterogeneity is not a second-order nuisance but a first-order property of the problem: clients differ systematically in their local data distributions, and therefore in their local loss curvature, and convergence analyses that ignore this are loose exactly where FL is deployed. On the other side, the communication-compression literature -- gradient quantization, sparsification, and error feedback [19][20][21][22] -- has produced mature tools for shrinking transmitted payloads, but those tools are aimed at FIRST-ORDER payloads (gradients and model deltas); they compress vectors, not the curvature objects that dominate the cost of second-order FL. Sketched-Hessian communication therefore sits in a gap: it is the dominant cost, it is heterogeneity-sensitive in principle, and yet the existing compression machinery does not address it.

Against that backdrop, FedNS/FedNDES rest on a single load-bearing assumption that has gone unexamined: the sketch dimension and accuracy are set ONCE, globally and uniformly -- one effective dimension d~_lambda and one schedule eps_t -- and applied IDENTICALLY to every client. The stakes of this choice are quantitative, not cosmetic. The per-round communication is dominated by the k x M sketched block per client, and a single global k must be large enough for the hardest client, so a uniform sketch forces every client to the worst case. The method that exists specifically to economize the communication-convergence tradeoff thus spends a worst-case budget uniformly, in the one regime -- heterogeneous, non-IID clients -- that FL is built to serve.

## 2. Key Findings and Analysis

We name this gap HAZE -- Heterogeneity-Agnostic Sketch Equalization: the practice of equalizing a single oblivious sketch budget across clients whose curvature is not equal. The defect is structural, not a tuning artifact. FedNS/FedNDES choose the sketch size from a single global effective dimension d~_lambda and a single accuracy schedule eps_t, but in non-IID FL each client j has its own local Hessian spectrum and therefore its own local effective dimension d~_lambda,j = tr(H_j(H_j+lambda I)^{-1}). One budget is being applied to many spectra. The mismatch is two-sided and unavoidable under a uniform rule: on spectrally-simple clients the allocated sketch dimension overshoots (m_j > d~_lambda,j), so those clients transmit k x M blocks whose extra rows carry essentially no curvature information -- pure wasted uplink bytes; on spectrally-rich clients the same uniform size undershoots (m_j < d~_lambda,j), so the local sketch fails to resolve the client's curvature and injects sketch error into its block.

The undershoot is the more damaging half because of how the federated Newton step is assembled. The global Hessian is a sum of locals, H = sum_j H_j, and the FedNS update is recovered by summing the locally sketched factors sum_j S^t_j H^{1/2}_{Dj,t}. Sketch error on any single spectrally-rich client therefore does not stay local: it propagates into the SHARED global step that every client then descends. A uniform budget that is comfortable for the median client can thus be silently corrupted by its richest clients, slowing the global convergence that the second-order method was adopted to accelerate. Raising the uniform k to protect those clients only widens the overshoot everywhere else, inflating the dominant k x M payload across the entire population. Under a uniform rule there is no setting of k that is simultaneously cheap for the simple clients and accurate for the rich ones.

What makes HAZE a SILENT loss is that neither of its two costs shows up as a failure. The over-communication hides inside a fixed total budget -- the bytes are spent, the curve still descends, and nothing flags that a fraction of every payload was informationless. The under-resolution hides inside an otherwise-healthy convergence curve -- the method still converges, just from strictly inside the achievable frontier, and without a heterogeneity-aware baseline there is nothing to reveal that a cheaper or faster operating point existed. Because both costs are invisible at the level of a single training run, a uniform-sketch method can look optimal while leaving a systematic, quantifiable gap on the table.

This yields a falsifiable prediction that organizes the rest of this work. The size of the HAZE gap should scale with the DISPERSION of local effective dimensions d~_lambda,j across clients. When dispersion is high -- distinct local spectra, the regime non-IID FL specifically targets -- FedNS/FedNDES must sit strictly inside the achievable communication-vs-convergence frontier, because a single budget cannot match many spectra at once. When dispersion is zero -- homogeneous clients with a common d~_lambda -- the uniform choice is already correct, the gap closes, and any heterogeneity-aware method must coincide with FedNS exactly. The gap is therefore not a constant overhead but a function of heterogeneity, peaking precisely where the method is deployed and vanishing where the assumption happens to hold.

The analysis points directly at the corrective lever. If a single global budget is the cause, the remedy is to let the sketch budget vary with the curvature it is sketching -- to allocate per-client sketch sizes m_j as a function of each client's local effective dimension d~_lambda,j, reallocating bytes away from spectrally-simple clients and toward spectrally-rich ones under the same total uplink budget. Such a spectrum-matched allocation should recover the frontier gap exactly in the high-dispersion regime while, by construction, reducing to the uniform Gaussian sketch -- and thus to FedNS's exact update and convergence guarantee -- as dispersion goes to zero. This is the design FedSANS (Federated Spectrum-Adaptive Newton Sketch) is built to realize, and the formulation that follows specifies it and puts the dispersion-scaling prediction to test.

## 3. Novel Research Idea (5-Question Formulation)

**[Question 1] - What is the problem?**

In FedNS/FedNDES-style second-order federated learning, does decomposing the single global effective dimension d~_lambda and uniform accuracy schedule eps_t into per-client, spectrum-adaptive sketch sizes m_j driven by each client's local effective dimension d~_lambda,j and allocated under a fixed total uplink budget B = sum_j m_j x M improve the communication-vs-convergence tradeoff over FedNS/FedNDES on non-IID FEMNIST, given that the method provably recovers FedNS's update and convergence guarantee when clients are homogeneous and sketches revert to uniform Gaussian at the worst-case size?

**[Question 2] - Why is it interesting and important?**

The communication-vs-convergence tradeoff is the defining constraint of federated learning (FL): since FedAvg [1], the dominant cost is not local compute but the bytes exchanged per round, and reducing rounds-to-target-accuracy under a bounded uplink is the field's central optimization target. Second-order/sketched methods are a fast-rising answer to this problem: by transmitting a sketched square-root Hessian rather than the full curvature, FedNS/FedNDES [9] inherit Newton-Sketch's [10] near-quadratic local geometry and achieve, for the first time, superlinear convergence in communication rounds for FL -- a qualitatively stronger round-complexity than first-order methods [1][2][3], with the per-round payload controlled by the Hessian's effective dimension d~_lambda = tr(H(H+lambda I)^{-1}) [11][13]. This is exactly why heterogeneity-blind sketch sizing matters. First, the very regime FL targets is non-IID: heterogeneity is a first-order driver of FL behavior, not a second-order nuisance [2][3][5][6][7], so client Hessian spectra -- and therefore the local effective dimensions d~_lambda,j -- are genuinely dispersed. FedNS/FedNDES collapse this dispersion into a single global d~_lambda and a single accuracy schedule eps_t = 1/log(1+t) applied with a shared sketch size k >~ M to every client; a data-oblivious, client-uniform sketch necessarily over-communicates on spectrally-simple clients and under-resolves spectrally-rich ones, leaving the method strictly inside its own achievable communication frontier precisely when clients are heterogeneous. Second, the proposed fix -- FedSANS, setting each m_j as an increasing function of the local d~_lambda,j and allocating under a fixed total uplink budget B = sum_j m_j x M -- converts the wasted bytes into resolution where curvature is actually rich, improving the tradeoff curve (test accuracy / optimality gap vs cumulative uplink bytes at a fixed delta-target) without opening a new bulk channel or changing the sketched-Hessian payload type: it only reallocates the sketch budget FedNS/FedNDES already spend (the sole addition being a per-client scalar curvature statistic, whose privacy treatment we flag as future work). Third, the broader implications are structural rather than point-wise. (i) A spectrum-matched budget rule is a contribution to a method family: as a PREDICTED CONSEQUENCE (a hypothesis pending cross-method validation, not a demonstrated result here), any sketched-Hessian or sketched-second-order FL procedure that exposes a per-client sketch size as an allocatable resource could adopt the same per-client sizing. (ii) It directly lowers real uplink cost at fixed accuracy, which is the binding constraint in cross-device, cross-silo, and edge deployments where the sketched-Hessian payload (~k x M per client) dominates the round. (iii) Because m_j is read off an interpretable local curvature statistic, the allocation doubles as a per-client curvature-budget audit -- a transparent account of which clients consume communication and why. Together these reframe effective-dimension-aware communication budgeting as a first-class design axis for second-order FL, shifting the question from "how large a uniform sketch?" to "how should a fixed byte budget be split across heterogeneous local spectra?", and motivating future work on adaptive, dispersion-driven budgeting (and its interaction with generalization across clients [8]).

**[Question 3] - Why is it hard?**

Allocating a fixed per-round uplink budget B = sum_j m_j x M by local spectrum is hard because several difficulties compound: (a) the per-client local effective dimension d~_lambda,j = tr(H_j (H_j + lambda I)^{-1}) is NOT directly observable and must be estimated communication-free and WITHOUT forming or storing the full local Hessian H_j -- only Hessian-vector products (HVPs) are available [12] -- so the allocation rests on a stochastic-trace estimator whose bias/variance at few probes can mis-rank clients and corrupt the split; (b) the allocation is COUPLED through the shared budget -- bytes given to client j are taken from the rest, and the optimal split depends on how a client's sketch error propagates into the GLOBAL Newton step, which is non-separable because the global Hessian H = sum_j H_j is a SUM of local Hessians, so per-client error does not decompose additively into global step error; (c) preserving the guarantee is delicate -- FedNS's (super)linear proof assumes a UNIFORM per-iteration sketch accuracy eps_t = 1/log(1+t), and with heterogeneous m_j one must show the AGGREGATE sketch still satisfies the global worst-direction accuracy needed for delta-accurate convergence, since naive strictly-proportional sizing can starve a spectrally-rich client below the worst-direction floor and break the rate; (d) non-stationarity -- d~_lambda,j drifts as w_t moves and H_j changes across rounds, so a one-shot allocation is stale and a cheap, budget-respecting re-estimation schedule is required; and (e) causal attribution/falsifiability -- handing more bytes to "hard" clients could help for a trivial reason (more total effective curvature resolved), so the gain must be shown to arise from SPECTRUM-MATCHING and not from incidental budget reshuffling. It is nonetheless TRACTABLE: HVPs are cheap and already intrinsic to Newton-sketch methods; the Hutchinson/stochastic-Lanczos estimator [17] converges fast for the bounded ratio tr(H(H+lambda I)^{-1}) in [0, M]; the collapse identity bounds downside risk to "how far above the FedNS floor" we land; and small regularized-logistic / shallow-net testbeds with well-defined Hessians keep the full estimate-allocate-sketch-aggregate pipeline runnable on a single GPU.

**[Question 4] - Why hasn't it been solved before?**

FedSANS's novelty is a conjunction of three clauses that NO existing federated-Newton or sketched-second-order method satisfies simultaneously: (a) per-client sketch size m_j set from the local effective dimension d~_lambda,j (not a global d~_lambda or uniform eps_t); (b) allocation of those sizes under a fixed total uplink budget B = sum_j m_j x M -- a budget-constrained heterogeneous split that reallocates bytes from spectrally-simple to spectrally-rich clients; and (c) a provable collapse to FedNS's exact centralized-Newton update and its (super)linear convergence guarantee at the homogeneous degenerate configuration (all d~_lambda,j equal, sketches reverting to uniform Gaussian at worst-case size). Every near-miss in the literature covers at most one clause. FedNS/FedNDES [9] is the closest prior method but is data-oblivious and client-uniform: it has the federated/collapse structure but lacks both (a) and (b). Centralized effective-dimension-adaptive Newton sketching [11] adapts sketch size to d~_lambda -- partially clause (a) -- but is single-machine, with no per-client federated budget split (no b) and no FL collapse identity (no c). Data-aware sketching via leverage scores [10][14], determinantal/RandNLA constructions [15], and Nystrom approximations [16] make the sketch curvature-sensitive, yet none is federated or allocates a shared byte budget across clients (no b, no c). Gradient/model-compression methods in FL -- QSGD [19], signSGD [20], error-feedback [21], top-k, and FetchSGD's count-sketch of gradients [22] -- compress FIRST-ORDER payloads under a budget but never size a CURVATURE sketch by effective dimension (no a, and the budgeted object is the wrong one). Heterogeneity-aware FL -- FedProx [2], SCAFFOLD [3], and system/resource-heterogeneity methods such as HeteroFL [4] -- handle data or device heterogeneity but act on first-order updates and never match a sketch size to a client's curvature spectrum (no a, no b in the curvature sense). Even classical distributed-Newton methods like DANE [18] aggregate local second-order information without per-client effective-dimension budgeting (no a, no b). Two STRUCTURAL reasons explain why the gap persisted, rather than mere oversight. First, effective-dimension-adaptive sketching theory is recent and centralized: the size-from-d~_lambda result [11] dates only to 2021 and was developed for single-machine Newton sketching, so the conceptual ingredient for clause (a) simply did not exist in a federated form until FedNS/FedNDES [9] ported sketched Newton to FL in 2024. Second, FL communication research concentrated almost entirely on first-order gradient compression [19][20][21][22]; per-client CURVATURE budgeting is meaningless until a method makes the per-client sketch size an explicit, allocatable resource -- which only happens once a sketched-second-order FL method (FedNS/FedNDES) transmits a sketched Hessian whose payload (~k x M per client) is the dominant, divisible cost. FedSANS differs by being the first to (a) read m_j off the local d~_lambda,j (estimable via Hessian-vector products [12] and stochastic trace estimation [17]), (b) solve the budget-constrained split B = sum_j m_j x M across heterogeneous clients, and (c) prove that this reduces EXACTLY to FedNS at zero dispersion. The collapse identity (c) is what keeps the positioning honest: FedSANS claims no free lunch -- at zero spectral dispersion it is FedNS, so any measured advantage must come solely from heterogeneity and must grow with the dispersion of local effective dimensions, never from a different payload, channel, or relaxed privacy model.

**[Question 5] - What are the key components of my approach and results?**

FORMALIZATION.
Local effective dimension: d~_lambda,j = tr( H_j (H_j + lambda I)^{-1} ), with H_j the local (regularized) Hessian at the current iterate w_t and M = feature/param dimension, so 0 <= d~_lambda,j <= M. Per-client sketch S^t_j of size m_j x n_j applied to the local square-root Hessian gives the block S^t_j H^{1/2}_{Dj,t}; the global sketched Hessian is the concatenation/sum sum_j S^t_j H^{1/2}_{Dj,t} (unchanged payload TYPE vs FedNS). Budget constraint: B = sum_j m_j x M (uplink bytes/round). Closed-form allocation rule: m_j = clip( round( (B/M) * d~_lambda,j / sum_i d~_lambda,i ), m_min, M ). Default sketch is Gaussian (data-OBLIVIOUS) so the collapse identity is clean; an OPTIONAL data-aware variant (leverage-score / Nystrom) is offered as an ablation only, never as the default.

ESTIMATOR.
Communication-free estimate of d~_lambda,j by Hutchinson stochastic trace estimation of tr(H_j(H_j+lambda I)^{-1}): draw p Rademacher/Gaussian probes z, form (H_j+lambda I)^{-1} H_j z via HVPs + a few CG/Lanczos inner steps, average z^T (.) z. Cost O(p x (HVP cost)) per client, no Hessian materialized. Variance controlled by probe count p and stochastic-Lanczos quadrature; WARM-START the estimate across rounds (previous-round value as prior) to cut p. Only a SCALAR d~_lambda,j leaves the client.

ALLOCATION + RE-ESTIMATION SCHEDULE.
Each participating client uploads one float (its d~_lambda,j estimate; negligible comm). Server normalizes, applies the clip rule, and broadcasts the integer m_j (also negligible, downlink). Re-estimate every R rounds (else reuse warm-started value). Budget conservation: post-clip renormalization redistributes any floor/cap slack so that sum_j m_j x M <= B exactly each round.

COLLAPSE UNIT TEST (B1).
Degenerate config: force all d~_lambda,j equal and set every m_j = k at the worst-case Gaussian size. Assert sum_j S^t_j H^{1/2}_{Dj,t} reproduces FedNS's S^t H^{1/2}_{D,t} and the resulting Newton step to tol 1e-6 (index/identity equality). FedSANS must then inherit FedNS's eps_t = 1/log(1+t) (super)linear rate. Any divergence is a BUG, not a contribution -- this is the honesty anchor.

DATASETS / MODELS.
(1) Synthetic Gaussian-design ridge / regularized-logistic with CONTROLLABLE per-client spectra (set d~_lambda,j and dispersion exactly) -- the clean dose-response bed. (2) Regularized logistic regression and a small 2-layer MLP / small CNN where the Hessian / effective dimension is well-defined. (3) Non-IID FEMNIST (LEAF) for the headline result; plus an MNIST-class / tabular logistic setup. Regimes: cross-silo (tens of clients, full participation) and cross-device (hundreds, partial participation).

METRICS.
PRIMARY: communication-to-accuracy TRADEOFF curve -- global test accuracy and optimality gap (L(w_t) - L*) vs CUMULATIVE UPLINK BYTES at a fixed delta-accuracy target. SECONDARY: rounds-to-delta-accuracy; per-client byte-allocation audit (m_j vs d~_lambda,j); sketch-error vs d~_lambda,j diagnostic (verifies error tracks spectral richness); per-client and WORST-CLIENT test accuracy, reported as the worst-client accuracy gap (max_j L_j(w_T) - min_j L_j(w_T)) across Dirichlet heterogeneity levels, to verify that the budget reallocation does not systematically degrade minority-distribution clients whose low local effective dimension yields a smaller sketch share.

BASELINES.
FedNS and FedNDES (uniform sketch, PRIMARY comparators). FedAvg / FedProx / SCAFFOLD (first-order references at MATCHED bytes). GIANT / DANE (distributed Newton) where applicable. UNIFORM-budget FedSANS ablation (same total B, equal split) to isolate the adaptive mechanism.

FALSIFIABILITY TRIAD.
(i) COLLAPSE B1 must PASS (exact to 1e-6; recovers FedNS rate). (ii) DUAL PLACEBO -- Leg-1 (structure-destroying): allocate the SAME total B by a RANDOM per-client split (identical bytes, mechanism destroyed) must NOT beat uniform FedNS; if it does, the gain is budget not spectrum-matching => FALSIFIED. Leg-2 (absent-mechanism): zero dispersion (all d~_lambda,j equal) must EQUAL FedNS within noise (the collapse in experiment form). (iii) DOSE-RESPONSE -- sweep dispersion via Dirichlet label-skew alpha and/or local Hessian condition-number spread; predict (FedSANS minus FedNS) advantage MONOTONE-increasing in dispersion and ZERO at zero dispersion. ABLATIONS: proportional vs water-filling allocation; re-estimation period R; probe count p; Gaussian vs leverage-score sketch; m_min / cap sensitivity.

EXPECTED OUTCOMES.
At equal cumulative uplink bytes, a MODEST-to-CLEAR reduction in bytes-to-delta-accuracy (target ~1.2x-2x fewer uplink bytes to reach the same accuracy) that GROWS with heterogeneity dispersion and VANISHES at zero dispersion; collapse exact; both placebo legs behave as predicted; allocation audit shows m_j tracking d~_lambda,j. FALSIFICATION CONDITION (crisp): the proposal is FALSIFIED if the random-split placebo matches/beats FedSANS at equal B, OR the advantage fails to vanish at zero dispersion, OR it is non-monotone in dispersion, OR B1 collapse does not hold to tol.

LIMITS (honest).
Theory and headline claims are convex / quadratic-friendly first (logistic, ridge, shallow nets with well-defined positive-(semi)definite Hessians where d~_lambda,j is meaningful). Deep nonconvex regimes -- indefinite Hessians, Gauss-Newton/Fisher surrogates, drift under large steps -- are FUTURE WORK; the collapse identity and budget guardrail (reallocation only, same sketched-Hessian payload type, no new bulk channel) bound the downside throughout. One honest privacy caveat: FedSANS does introduce a single new uplink signal absent from FedNS -- the scalar d~_lambda,j per client, a curvature statistic of local private data -- so the no-privacy-relaxation claim holds for the bulk sketched-Hessian payload but not for that scalar; future work should analyze whether it requires differential-privacy noise (eps-DP) or secure-aggregation before deployment in sensitive cross-device settings.

## 4. References and Evidence

[1] McMahan, B., Moore, E., Ramage, D., Hampson, S., Aguera y Arcas, B. "Communication-Efficient Learning of Deep Networks from Decentralized Data." AISTATS, 2017. (FedAvg / LocalSGD)

[2] Li, T., Sahu, A. K., Zaheer, M., Sanjabi, M., Talwalkar, A., Smith, V. "Federated Optimization in Heterogeneous Networks." MLSys, 2020. (FedProx)

[3] Karimireddy, S. P., Kale, S., Mohri, M., Reddi, S. J., Stich, S. U., Suresh, A. T. "SCAFFOLD: Stochastic Controlled Averaging for Federated Learning." ICML, 2020.

[4] Diao, E., Ding, J., Tarokh, V. "HeteroFL: Computation and Communication Efficient Federated Learning for Heterogeneous Clients." ICLR, 2021.

[5] Su, L., Xu, J., Yang, P. "A Non-parametric View of FedAvg and FedProx: Beyond Stationary Points." arXiv:2106.15216, 2021.

[6] Wei, B., Li, J., Liu, Y., Wang, W. "Federated Learning for Non-IID Data: From Theory to Algorithm." PRICAI, 2021. (Springer LNCS)

[7] Wei, B., Li, J., Liu, Y., Wang, W. "Non-IID Federated Learning With Sharper Risk Bound." IEEE Transactions on Neural Networks and Learning Systems (TNNLS), 2022.

[8] Yuan, H., Morningstar, W. R., Ning, L., Singhal, K. "What Do We Mean by Generalization in Federated Learning?" ICLR, 2022.

[9] Li, J., Liu, Y., Wang, W., Wu, H., Wang, W. "FedNS: A Fast Sketching Newton-Type Algorithm for Federated Learning." AAAI, 2024. (arXiv:2401.02734 -- target method; FedNS + FedNDES)

[10] Pilanci, M., Wainwright, M. J. "Newton Sketch: A Near Linear-Time Optimization Algorithm with Linear-Quadratic Convergence." SIAM Journal on Optimization, 2017.

[11] Lacotte, J., Wang, Y., Pilanci, M. "Adaptive Newton Sketch: Linear-time Optimization with Quadratic Convergence and Effective Hessian Dimensionality." ICML, 2021.

[12] Schraudolph, N. N. "Fast Curvature Matrix-Vector Products for Second-Order Gradient Descent." Neural Computation, 14(7):1723-1738, 2002.

[13] Smale, S., Zhou, D.-X. "Learning Theory Estimates via Integral Operators and Their Approximations." Constructive Approximation, 26(2):153-172, 2007.

[14] Drineas, P., Magdon-Ismail, M., Mahoney, M. W., Woodruff, D. P. "Fast Approximation of Matrix Coherence and Statistical Leverage." Journal of Machine Learning Research, 2012.

[15] Derezinski, M., Mahoney, M. W. "Determinantal Point Processes in Randomized Numerical Linear Algebra." Notices of the American Mathematical Society, 2021.

[16] Williams, C. K. I., Seeger, M. "Using the Nystrom Method to Speed Up Kernel Machines." NeurIPS (NIPS), 2001.

[17] Hutchinson, M. F. "A Stochastic Estimator of the Trace of the Influence Matrix for Laplacian Smoothing Splines." Communications in Statistics - Simulation and Computation, 1990.

[18] Shamir, O., Srebro, N., Zhang, T. "Communication-Efficient Distributed Optimization using an Approximate Newton-type Method." ICML, 2014. (DANE)

[19] Alistarh, D., Grubic, D., Li, J., Tomioka, R., Vojnovic, M. "QSGD: Communication-Efficient SGD via Gradient Quantization and Encoding." NeurIPS, 2017.

[20] Bernstein, J., Wang, Y.-X., Azizzadenesheli, K., Anandkumar, A. "signSGD: Compressed Optimisation for Non-Convex Problems." ICML, 2018.

[21] Karimireddy, S. P., Rebjock, Q., Stich, S. U., Jaggi, M. "Error Feedback Fixes SignSGD and other Gradient Compression Schemes." ICML, 2019.

[22] Rothchild, D., Panda, A., Ullah, E., Ivkin, N., Stoica, I., Braverman, V., Gonzalez, J., Arora, R. "FetchSGD: Communication-Efficient Federated Learning with Sketching." ICML, 2020.
